title: A rollup-centric ethereum roadmap
quote: L2 will serve as a branded shard of Ethereum; rollups become the primary scaling solution for the near to mid-term future.

title: Vitalik Buterin reevaluates Ethereum's rollup-centric roadmap
quote: Vitalik Buterin appears to have given up on the 'rollup-centric' scaling model he previously championed, noting that Layer 2s have decentralized 'far slower' than anticipated while the Ethereum base layer scaled. 'Both of these facts mean that the original vision of L2s and their role in Ethereum no longer makes sense, and we need a new path,' Buterin said. He suggests viewing them as a broad spectrum of chains with varying degrees of connection to Ethereum.

title: EIP-4844: Shard Blob Transactions
quote: Introduces a new transaction format for 'blob-carrying transactions' which contain a large amount of data that cannot be accessed by EVM execution, but whose commitment can be accessed.

title: Fusaka Mainnet Announcement
quote: Fusaka activated on the Ethereum mainnet on December 3, 2025. At Fusaka activation, blob limits remained at 6/9 (target/max). Staged BPO forks raise blob count: BPO1 to 10/15; BPO2 to 14/21 on January 7, 2026.

title: EIP-7594: Peer Data Availability Sampling
quote: Introduces a new networking protocol that allows nodes to verify blob data availability through sampling rather than downloading entire blobs. Theoretically enables scaling blob space to ~8x the current size; long-term roadmap targets ~128 blobs per slot at maturity.

title: EIP-4844: The Upgrade That Made L2s Actually Affordable
quote: Before EIP-4844, L2 transaction costs were broken down as L2 execution costing $0.02 (cheap) and L1 data posting costing $2.45 (expensive), with 98% of the cost being storage data on Ethereum. Implemented in the Dencun upgrade (March 2024), it created a separate fee market for L2 rollups, reducing L2 transaction costs by 10-100x while maintaining Ethereum's security guarantees.

title: L2BEAT Stages framework
quote: Stage 0 represents a project fully controlled by few entities; Stage 2 represents a project fully controlled by code; Stage 1 sits in between. Arbitrum, OP Mainnet, and Base all have live, permissionless fraud proof systems and are now classified as Stage 1. Most L2 networks remain more centralized than they appear.

title: The different types of ZK-EVMs
quote: Type 1 zkEVMs are identical to Ethereum at the bytecode level — the holy grail but slowest to generate proofs. Higher-numbered types are less compatible but faster. Trade Ethereum compatibility for proof speed.

title: The Evolution of zkEVMs: Balancing Compatibility and Performance in Ethereum Scaling
quote: Scroll and Linea take the Type 2 path. zkSync Era and StarkNet represent the Type 4 approach (zkSync transpiles Solidity to custom bytecode optimized for ZK). By 2026, proving times have collapsed from 16 minutes to 16 seconds, costs have dropped 45x. Multiple teams have demonstrated real-time proof generation faster than Ethereum's 12-second block times.

title: MegaETH | The First Real-Time Blockchain (research page)
quote: MegaETH is real-time Ethereum, streaming transactions at lightning speed: sub-millisecond latency and over 100,000 transactions per second. Sequencer nodes are equipped with abundant RAM to hold the entire blockchain state (~100GB). Decouples sequencing, execution, and validation across heterogeneous nodes; uses SALT (Small Authentication Large Trie), a write-optimized storage backend, and JIT compilation. Mini-blocks every 10ms; standard EVM blocks every 1s.

title: MegaETH: The Real-Time Blockchain Revolutionizing Speed and Scalability
quote: MegaETH launched its mainnet on January 22, 2026, processed 10.7 billion transactions during a seven-day stress test, achieving sustained throughput of 35,000 TPS, with block times that dropped from 400ms to just 10ms.

title: Monad First Look: Hyperscaling the EVM
quote: Monad uses parallel execution and optimizations to scale to 10,000+ TPS with sub-second finality while retaining support for a relatively large validator set. Optimistic concurrency: predicts non-overlapping state access, executes transactions in parallel, and replays only the conflicting subset. MonadDB optimized for fine-grained concurrency. Targets 10k+ TPS, 400ms blocks, 800ms deterministic finality. Mainnet launched November 24, 2025.

title: Reth's path to 1 gigagas per second, and beyond
quote: Reth already achieves 100-200 mgas/s during live sync (including senders recovery, executing transactions, and calculating the trie on every block). Path is towards 1 gigagas per second in L2; longer-term roadmap goes beyond. Goal: ~10,000 TPS in the medium term.

title: What's New in Paradigm's Reth 2.0
quote: Reth 2.0 (released April 2026) can already process the equivalent of roughly 28 Ethereum mainnet blocks per second.

title: Reducing BAL Size with 10 GigaGas/s EVM Throughput in the Presence of I/O
quote: Parallel I/O BAL achieves ~10.8 GGas/s on a 16-core commodity machine under mega-block setting (January 2026).

title: Verkle trees | ethereum.org
quote: Verkle trees ('Vector commitment' + 'Merkle Trees') let nodes stop storing large amounts of state data without losing the ability to validate blocks — a critical step on the path to stateless Ethereum clients.

title: EIP-7736: Leaf-level state expiry in verkle trees
quote: Adds an 'update epoch' to the verkle tree extension node; when an epoch expires, extension and suffix nodes can be deleted. A new transaction type uses a simple verkle proof to pay for reactivating the extension/suffix nodes.

title: EIP-7864: Ethereum state using a unified binary tree
quote: Renewed binary tree proposal: a single balanced tree with packing of account data, storage-slot, and code chunks. Mid-2024 motivations include quantum-computing concerns about Verkle's vector commitments and revisiting binary tree options.

title: Finality - ZKsync Docs
quote: ZKsync's proof generation typically takes about an hour. The average time to hard finality (when batches are verified and finalized on L1) ranged from 10 to 20 minutes, depending on network congestion and prover availability.

title: 10 Takeaways from the Based Rollup Summit – Cannes 2025
quote: Taiko is the first based rollup scaling solution on Ethereum. Based Preconfirmation was originally proposed by Justin Drake. Justin Drake: 'we must scale horizontally; there is no other option. Even a second faster is not enough.' Preconfirming rollup transactions allows execution in milliseconds instead of waiting 12 seconds, with a subset of Ethereum validators opting in.

title: The 2026 Data Availability Race: Celestia, EigenDA, and Avail's Battle for Blockchain Scalability
quote: Celestia holds 50% DA market share, posted 160+ GB of data. Every major rollup framework — Arbitrum Orbit, OP Stack, Polygon CDK — supports Celestia. EigenDA throughput claim 100 MB/s vs Celestia's ~1.33 MB/s. When Ethereum L2s paid $3.83/MB on blobs, Eclipse paid Celestia $0.07/MB — 55x cheaper. EigenDA's DAC architecture enables higher throughput but introduces trust assumptions full-blockchain solutions avoid.

title: Choosing a Data Availability Layer: Avail vs Celestia vs EigenDA vs Ethereum
quote: Avail positions as the most flexible option for multichain applications with lower costs but lower economic security than Ethereum blobs.

title: The Espresso Sequencer
quote: The Espresso Sequencer is envisioned as a shared public good for both optimistic and zk-rollups that will allow rollups to decentralize while introducing interoperability advantages of a shared sequencing layer.

title: 2026 Layer 2 Outlook
quote: Astria, once a leading shared-sequencer effort, shut down entirely in 2025, underscoring how early and fragile this category still is. Shared-sequencer networks like Espresso have continued to develop, but adoption remains limited.

title: Fast, Configurable, and Verifiable Sequencing is Coming to Every OP Stack Chain
quote: In 2025, Base and Unichain offer their users lightning-fast confirmations via 200ms Flashblocks - a 10x upgrade over the standard 2s blocks supported by the OP Stack. SUAVE chain was archived in May 2025 as Flashbots pivoted to BuilderNet and Flashnet infrastructure.

title: State of the Proving Infrastructure Landscape - 2025Q1
quote: Succinct launched the level-1 version testnet of its prover network in February 2025. Boundless public testnet allows anyone with the right infrastructure to become a prover; 18 entities currently participate as provers.
